preFix.title: Top-dressing a lawn with compost
preFix.subtitle: Applying a thin layer of sieved compost to improve soil structure and reduce inputs
preFix.excerpt: A 5 to 10 mm top-dressing of sieved mature compost applied to a lawn in autumn or early spring improves soil biology, reduces compaction, and builds organic matter without the cost of fertiliser. This is the timing, preparation, and application method, with how much compost is needed per square metre.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS 'Top-dressing lawns' guidance. Sports Turf Research Institute (STRI) on compost amendments for amenity turf. No-dig movement literature (Charles Dowding) on compost as a soil builder. Garden Organic compost use factsheets.

content.text: A
topdressing
of mature compost feeds the soil biology, builds organic matter over years, and cuts fertiliser use. Applied in autumn, the compost works down through the
thatch
over winter and is in the roots by spring.
When to topdress
Autumn (September to October): the best time. The compost incorporates over winter, improving soil structure before spring growth.
Early spring (March to April): second-best. Apply after scarifying if scarifying is part of your spring programme. Spring topdressing supports early growth.
Avoid midsummer: applying compost to a dry lawn in July traps moisture in the grass surface and can cause scalding in hot conditions.
Preparing the compost

Only use fully mature, fine-screened compost for topdressing. Sieve through a 10 mm mesh to remove twigs, stones, and lumps. Any partially decomposed material left on the lawn surface becomes a thatch problem rather than solving one. The material should be moist but not wet; dry powdery compost blows away, wet compost clogs the grass.
How much compost per square metre

A 5 mm topdressing layer requires 5 litres per square metre. A 10 mm layer requires 10 litres per square metre. For a 50 m² lawn, a 5 mm topdressing needs 250 litres (approximately 0.25 m³) of finished compost. This is a large volume; if your heap has not produced this much finished material, use a 3 mm topdressing and supplement with fine sand for a lighter application.
Application method
Mow the lawn to 30 to 40 mm before topdressing, lower than the normal summer cutting height, to allow the compost to make contact with the soil below the grass blade.
Scatter the sieved compost by hand or shovel, working in sections. Aim for an even depth of 5 to 10 mm across the whole area.

Work the compost into the grass with the back of a rake or a stiff lawn brush, using a dragging and distributing motion. The compost should drop down through the grass blades rather than sitting on top of them.
Water lightly if rain is not expected within 24 hours. The compost needs to be moist enough to continue decomposing in contact with the soil.
What to expect

After an autumn topdressing, the lawn may look patchy for two to three weeks as the compost settles. By spring, the soil beneath the topdressed area is darker, more friable, and supports stronger root development. Maintained annually for three to five years, compost topdressing transforms a compacted, patchy lawn into a resilient sward that needs half the synthetic fertiliser input.
